content.text:
Fermat's and Euler's theorems have many applications beyond pure 
number theory.

Computing $a^k \pmod{n}$ directly would require $k-1$ multiplications. 
The method of **repeated squaring** is 
much faster.

content.title: Repeated Squaring
content.content:
To compute $a^k \pmod{n}$:

1. Write $k$ in binary: $k = \sum_{i} b_i 2^i$.

2. Compute $a^{2^i} \pmod{n}$ by repeated squaring.

3. Multiply together the terms where $b_i = 1$.

content.text:
When computing $a^k \pmod{n}$, we can first reduce $k$ modulo $\phi(n)$ 
(assuming $\gcd(a, n) = 1$).

content.title: Exponent Reduction
content.problem: Find the last two digits of $3^{1000}$.
content.solution:
We need $3^{1000} \pmod{100}$. Since $\gcd(3, 100) = 1$ and 
$\phi(100) = 40$, we have $3^{40} \equiv 1 \pmod{100}$.

$1000 = 40 \cdot 25$, so $3^{1000} \equiv 1 \pmod{100}$.

The last two digits are 01.

content.text:
Modular exponentiation forms the basis of some pseudorandom number 
generators. The **Blum-Blum-Shub generator** 
uses $x_{n+1} = x_n^2 \pmod{n}$ where $n = pq$ for primes 
$p \equiv q \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$.

content.title: Divisibility by 7
content.solution:
$10 \equiv 3 \pmod{7}$ and $\phi(7) = 6$, so $10^6 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$.

$100 = 6 \cdot 16 + 4$, so $10^{100} \equiv 3^4 = 81 \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$.

Thus $10^{100} + 1 \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$, so 7 does not divide $10^{100} + 1$.

content.title: Decimal Period
content.content:
Let $p$ be a prime other than 2 or 5. The decimal expansion of $1/p$ 
has period dividing $\text{ord}_p(10)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The decimal expansion of $1/p$ repeats when remainders repeat in long 
division. The remainder after $k$ steps is $10^k \bmod p$. This repeats 
when $10^k \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.

content.title: Decimal Period of $1/7$
content.problem:
$\text{ord}_7(10) = 6$ (since $10^6 \equiv 1$ but no smaller power works).

Thus $1/7 = 0.\overline{142857}$ has period 6.
